title: Heatwave: No relief till May 24 - MillenniumPost
full_text:
Heatwave: No relief till May 24
New Delhi: Large parts of northwest and central India are set to face a prolonged spell of high temperatures this week, with the mercury already nearing 45 degrees Celsius in several locations on Monday. The India Meteorological Department has warned that heatwave conditions will persist across multiple states until May 24, with some areas likely to see even more severe conditions in the coming days.
According to the weather office, isolated pockets of Punjab, Haryana, Rajasthan and Delhi are expected to experience heatwave conditions through the week. In Uttar Pradesh, both western and eastern regions may see heatwave to severe heatwave conditions starting Tuesday. Similar conditions are also likely across parts of Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Vidarbha and Marathwada during different days in the same period. Isolated areas in Bihar, Telangana, Coastal Andhra Pradesh and Yanam are also expected to be affected.
Temperatures have already climbed sharply across several regions. In Punjab, Bathinda recorded a high of 47 degrees Celsius, among the highest in the country. In neighbouring Haryana, Sirsa registered 46.2 degrees Celsius. Other parts of Haryana also saw high temperatures, with Rohtak at 45.2 degrees Celsius, Hisar at 44.4 degrees Celsius and Narnaul at 44.3 degrees Celsius. Gurugram recorded 42 degrees Celsius.
In Rajasthan, several cities crossed 45 degrees Celsius. Pilani and Chittorgarh both recorded 46.2 degrees Celsius, while Sri Ganganagar touched 46.1 degrees. Bikaner recorded 46 degrees Celsius, followed by Vanasthali at 45.6 degrees, Fatehpur at 45.5 degrees and Kota at 45.3 degrees. Barmer and Churu recorded 45.1 degrees each. Jaipur reported 43.6 degrees Celsius, with most cities in the state crossing 41 degrees.
The Meteorological Centre in Jaipur said the weather is likely to remain dry across Rajasthan through the week. Heatwave conditions are expected in border areas of western Rajasthan and parts of the south-eastern region. Strong dusty winds moving at speeds of 20 to 30 kilometres per hour are also expected across western, northern and eastern parts of the state over the next four to five days.
Delhi also experienced high temperatures, with some areas touching 44 degrees Celsius. The Safdarjung base station recorded a maximum temperature of 43.4 degrees Celsius. The Ridge station was the hottest at 44.6 degrees Celsius, while Lodhi Road recorded 43.8 degrees, which is 4.8 degrees above normal. This marked the hottest May day in the city in two years.Dry winds and intense heat made daytime conditions difficult for commuters in the national capital. Mahesh Palawat, vice president at Skymet Weather, said, “The northwesterly winds are sweeping into the national capital from the Thar Desert region of Rajasthan and parts of central Pakistan. Travelling across vast arid stretches, these winds turn intensely dry by the time they reach Delhi, trapping heat close to the surface and sharply intensifying the searing conditions across the city.”
He added that night temperatures have remained high due to strong daytime heating and said there is no forecast of thunderstorms or pre-monsoon rainfall activity over the next 10 days. For Tuesday, the IMD has issued a yellow alert for heatwave conditions in Delhi, with temperatures likely to touch 45 degrees Celsius over the next two days.
The rising temperatures have also pushed up electricity demand in the capital. Peak power demand reached 7,542 megawatts, the highest so far this summer. The all-time high for Delhi stands at 8,656 megawatts, recorded two years ago.
In Punjab and Haryana, Chandigarh recorded its hottest day of the season so far at 42 degrees Celsius. Faridkot registered 46.4 degrees Celsius, while Amritsar recorded 42 degrees, Ludhiana 42.2 degrees and Patiala 44 degrees.
In contrast, hill states have seen relatively moderate temperatures but are also witnessing a rise. Dharamshala recorded a maximum of 35 degrees Celsius, while Manali and Shimla reported 28.4 degrees and 28.2 degrees respectively. Kufri recorded 23.8 degrees. Una was the hottest in Himachal Pradesh at 39.7 degrees Celsius, while Kukumseri recorded the lowest night temperature at 4.3 degrees.
The Shimla meteorological office has forecast a wet spell in Himachal Pradesh from May 19 to 24. A yellow warning has been issued for thunderstorms, lightning and gusty winds of 30 to 40 kilometres per hour in parts of Chamba, Kangra and Kullu on May 21, and in Chamba, Kangra, Kullu and Mandi on May 22.
The IMD said maximum temperatures are expected to rise gradually by 2 to 3 degrees Celsius across many parts of northwest India until May 24. In Chhattisgarh, temperatures may rise by 3 to 4 degrees Celsius till May 21. Maharashtra and Gujarat are likely to see no major change until May 18, followed by a slight drop of 2 to 3 degrees between May 19 and May 24. The rest of the country is expected to see no major change in temperatures during this period.
Amid the rising heat, the Directorate General of Health Services under the Union health ministry, in coordination with the Ayush ministry, has issued an advisory to help people cope with heat stress. The advisory recommends staying hydrated, avoiding direct sun exposure during afternoon hours, wearing light cotton clothing and consuming seasonal fruits and fluids rich in electrolytes.
It also advises precautions at workplaces and public spaces, including providing shaded rest areas, ensuring regular water breaks and raising awareness about signs of heat-related illness.
